fast.summary: This paper investigates using large multimodal models (LMMs) to predict engagement (likes, shares, etc.) for short-form user-generated videos by jointly processing video frames, text metadata, and audio. By ensembling VideoLLaMA2 and Qwen2.5-VL, the approach achieves first place in the ICCV VQualA 2025 EVQA-SnapUGC challenge.
fast.key_idea: Fine-tuning large multimodal models that natively fuse audio, visual, and language signals can outperform traditional hand-crafted multi-modal feature pipelines for video engagement prediction.
fast.why_it_matters: Accurate engagement prediction enables better content recommendation and creator guidance on short-video platforms; this work shows that off-the-shelf LMMs can serve as strong, generalizable baselines with minimal feature engineering.
possible_use_cases: Ranking and recommendation systems on short-video platforms (TikTok, Reels) to surface high-engagement content
Content creation assistance tools that score a video draft's predicted engagement before publishing
Ad placement optimization by predicting which user-generated videos will attract the most viewer interaction

deep.method_breakdown: The method fine-tunes two large multimodal models (VideoLLaMA2 and Qwen2.5-VL) on the SnapUGC dataset for regression-based video engagement prediction, framing it as a language generation task where the model outputs an engagement score. VideoLLaMA2 processes key video frames, text metadata (title, tags, description), and background audio jointly, while Qwen2.5-VL uses only frames and text. The final submission ensembles predictions from both model families to combine their complementary strengths.
deep.key_innovation: The primary novelty is repurposing general-purpose large multimodal models for video engagement prediction rather than training task-specific architectures. Prior work used hand-crafted or separately extracted features per modality with explicit fusion modules; here the LMM's pre-trained cross-modal attention implicitly handles cross-feature and cross-modality interactions end-to-end, eliminating the need for manually designed fusion strategies.
deep.technical_insight: The most important design choice is including audio as a modality input (via VideoLLaMA2) alongside visual and text. The ablation implied by comparing VideoLLaMA2 vs Qwen2.5-VL directly shows audio consistently improves performance, confirming that background sound carries engagement-relevant signals (music style, sound quality, speech) that visual and text features alone cannot capture. This validates that audio is a first-class modality for UGC engagement, not a secondary one.
deep.limitations: The approach is computationally expensive due to LMM inference at scale, making real-time deployment in production recommendation systems impractical. The models are trained and evaluated on SnapUGC, so generalization to other platforms (TikTok, YouTube Shorts) with different user demographics and content distributions is unvalidated. Engagement labels are platform- and time-specific (view counts decay/shift), so temporal distribution shift is a concern. The method also lacks interpretability — it is unclear which specific visual or audio cues drive score changes.
interview_questions: Why does framing engagement prediction as a language generation task work for LMMs, and what are the risks of this formulation?
The paper shows VideoLLaMA2 outperforms Qwen2.5-VL — how would you design a controlled ablation to isolate the contribution of audio versus the model architecture difference?
Engagement labels on UGC platforms are noisy and biased by recommendation feedback loops. How does this affect training and evaluation, and how would you mitigate it?
How would you adapt this approach for real-time serving given LMM inference latency, and what trade-offs would you accept?
The ensemble of two LMM families achieves first place — what does this suggest about model diversity, and how would you systematically construct a better ensemble?
interview_answers: LMMs can generate a scalar engagement score as a token sequence, leveraging their pre-trained cross-modal representations. The risk is that the model may hallucinate scores not grounded in the input or overfit to linguistic patterns in training prompts rather than genuine visual/audio signals; careful prompt design and regression loss calibration are critical.

You would need two models with identical architectures — one receiving audio+visual+text and one receiving visual+text only — trained on the same data splits. Comparing VideoLLaMA2 with and without its audio encoder (while keeping visual and language components fixed) isolates the audio contribution from architecture differences. The current paper conflates both variables.

Engagement labels reflect both content quality and algorithmic amplification, creating a feedback loop bias where popular-at-recommendation-time content gets inflated labels. Mitigation strategies include de-biasing labels by position/exposure normalization, using engagement rate (views/impressions) rather than raw counts, and temporal train/test splits to detect distribution shift rather than random splits.

Options include: (1) distill the LMM into a lightweight student model post-training, (2) cache LMM embeddings for seen videos and only run inference on new uploads, (3) use the LMM offline to generate engagement scores as features for a fast shallow model in the serving path. The trade-off is accuracy vs latency — the LMM would serve as a batch scorer rather than real-time ranker.

The ensemble's success implies the two model families make complementary errors, likely due to different pre-training data and architecture inductive biases. To build a better ensemble, you would analyze error correlation between models, add diversity through different input representations (e.g., different frame sampling strategies), and use learned stacking rather than simple averaging — training a meta-learner on out-of-fold predictions to optimally weight each model's contribution per input type.
